name: Scottie Scheffler
narrative: The most complete player in the world remains the man to beat at Augusta. Scheffler's iron play is borderline unfair on this course, and his two green jackets in three years tell you everything. His strokes gained approach numbers are historic, ranking first on the PGA Tour for three consecutive seasons. The field is chasing a ghost who seems to elevate his game at the precise moments that matter most. With three wins already in 2026, he arrives at Magnolia Lane in the form of his life and with the quiet confidence of a man who knows exactly how to navigate every inch of Augusta National.

whyTheyWillWin:
Scottie Scheffler's case for winning the 2026 Masters begins with a simple, almost unfair statistical reality: he is the best iron player in professional golf arriving at the most demanding second-shot course in championship golf. His strokes gained approach numbers have been historic for three consecutive seasons, and Augusta National rewards that skill above all others. When the pins are tucked on Sunday and the greens are firm and fast, Scheffler's ability to control trajectory, spin, and distance with his mid-irons gives him access to birdie opportunities that simply don't exist for the rest of the field.

His Augusta track record speaks volumes. Two green jackets in three years, with a T10 sandwiched in between, represents the kind of sustained dominance at one venue that we haven't seen since Tiger Woods. Scheffler doesn't just play well here -- he unlocks a different gear. His par-5 scoring at Augusta ranks among the best in tournament history, and his understanding of the course's subtleties has deepened with each visit. He knows when to attack and when to play to the fat side of the green, and that patience-aggression balance is the hallmark of every multiple Masters winner.

The 2026 season has been vintage Scheffler. Three wins in seven starts, a world ranking that continues to grow more secure, and a ball-striking package that is firing on all cylinders. His approach play is complemented by enough length off the tee to reach the par-5s in two with ease, and his scrambling has improved to the point where the short game is no longer a liability. At 29, Scheffler is in the absolute prime of his career, with the physical tools, mental maturity, and institutional knowledge to become just the fourth player to win three Masters titles before turning 30. The question isn't whether Scheffler can win -- it's whether anyone can stop him.
whyTheyWontWin:
The flat stick remains Scheffler's Achilles heel. Augusta National's greens are among the most treacherous in golf, and while elite iron play can set up birdie looks, you still have to make the putts. Scheffler's strokes gained putting hovers around Tour average, and in a week where the greens are running at 13 on the Stimpmeter, that gap between his ball-striking and his putting can be exposed. One bad putting round at Augusta can cost you the tournament, and Scheffler has had his share of cold stretches on the greens.

There's also the weight of expectations. Scheffler arrives as the overwhelming favorite at every major now, and while he has handled that burden remarkably well, history shows that even the greatest players go through stretches where the pressure of being the hunted takes a toll. The field knows his game inside and out, and the narrative of a three-peat at Augusta could become a psychological burden rather than a motivator. Additionally, his tendency toward conservative play when leading can allow aggressive closers like McIlroy or Schauffele to apply late pressure that creates uncomfortable moments.

name: Jon Rahm
narrative: Rahm's move to LIV hasn't dulled his edge at the majors -- his 2023 green jacket proved that. His combination of power and touch around Augusta's greens makes him a perpetual top-5 favorite. The limited reps against top fields are the only question mark, and even that feels like a minor quibble. Rahm's ball-striking remains elite by any measure, and his competitive fire burns as hot as anyone's in the field. The Spaniard's Seve-like flair for the dramatic, combined with modern power and precision, creates a player who is perfectly suited to Augusta's demands. With a T4 and T8 in his last two Masters starts, he has been remarkably consistent despite the LIV transition.

whyTheyWillWin:
Jon Rahm is perhaps the most naturally gifted player in the field at Augusta National, possessing a combination of power, touch, and competitive fire that few in the history of the game can match. His draw-dominant ball flight is perfectly suited to Augusta's layout, where multiple holes reward a right-to-left shape off the tee. Since his first Masters appearance in 2018, Rahm has posted a T5-or-better in five of eight starts -- a consistency that surpasses even Scheffler's record and suggests an almost preternatural fit between player and course.

Rahm's 2023 Masters victory was a masterclass in controlled aggression, and the lessons learned during that dominant wire-to-wire performance remain fresh. He understands the rhythm of the week, the way the course changes from Thursday to Sunday, and the precise moments where risk-taking is rewarded versus punished. His approach play, which ranks among the top five in the field, gives him the ability to attack pins that others must play away from, and his par-5 scoring has been elite throughout his Augusta career.

The LIV Golf question is frequently raised, but Rahm has answered it emphatically at the majors. His T4 and T8 at the last two Masters prove that his competitive edge hasn't dulled, and the reduced schedule may actually benefit him by providing freshness and hunger when he arrives at the biggest stages. Rahm approaches majors with the intensity of a player who has something to prove to the world, and that chip-on-the-shoulder mentality, combined with his immense talent, makes him one of the most dangerous players in the field regardless of where he plays the rest of the year.
whyTheyWontWin:
The fundamental concern with Rahm is preparation. Playing LIV Golf means fewer competitive rounds against the world's best players, fewer opportunities to test himself under the specific pressure of PGA Tour contention, and fewer chances to sharpen the fine edges of his game that separate winning from finishing top-5. While his major results have been strong, there's an argument that the gap between LIV competition and Masters Sunday will eventually catch up to him.

Rahm's emotional volatility, while often channeled productively, can also be his undoing. Augusta requires patience above all else -- the willingness to take your medicine, to play to the center of the green, to accept par when birdie isn't realistic. Rahm's aggressive instincts and competitive fire can occasionally override his better judgment, leading to the kind of high-risk decisions that produce big numbers. His T27 in 2022, his worst Masters finish, came during a week where his frustration visibly mounted. If the early rounds don't go his way, Rahm's temperament can become a liability rather than an asset.

name: Bryson DeChambeau
narrative: The Scientist has turned Augusta into a par-68 course with his driver, but the short game has always been his Achilles heel on these slick greens. Back-to-back US Open titles give him undeniable major pedigree. If the wedges cooperate, look out. His length advantage on the par-5s is among the most significant in the field, and his analytical approach to course management has only sharpened since joining LIV. The wildcard factor is real -- when everything clicks, he can shoot numbers nobody else in the field is capable of.

whyTheyWillWin:
Bryson DeChambeau has weaponized distance in a way that Augusta National simply was not designed to handle. His ability to reduce par-5s to drive-and-wedge propositions gives him a scoring advantage of nearly two strokes per round on those four holes alone. When he is hitting driver 340 yards down the middle of the 13th fairway and flipping a sand wedge onto the green, he is playing a different golf course than most of the field.

The two US Open titles -- including his stunning 2024 victory at Pinehurst No. 2 -- prove that DeChambeau can perform under the most intense pressure on the most demanding courses. His T10 in 2025 showed marked improvement in the areas that previously held him back at Augusta.

DeChambeau's analytical mind has been working on Augusta National as a puzzle since his amateur days. He has studied green slopes with the precision of an engineer, mapped wind patterns through the trees, and optimized his strategy for every pin position. His LIV schedule has given him more time to prepare specifically for majors.

The 2026 version of DeChambeau is more complete than any previous iteration. His wedge game has improved significantly. His putting on bermudagrass has prepared him for Augusta's overseeded greens. If the short game shows up, he has the firepower to blow the field away.
whyTheyWontWin:
Augusta National's greens remain DeChambeau's kryptonite. His strokes gained around the green is -0.05, and his 52.3% scrambling rate is a significant liability on a course where scrambling is essential.

The LIV factor cannot be ignored. He has not faced the sustained pressure of competing against Scheffler, Schauffele, and McIlroy on a regular basis. His tendency to get too analytical under pressure has cost him in past Masters, and Augusta punishes players who try to control every variable.

name: Rory McIlroy
narrative: The defending champion returns to Magnolia Lane having slain the dragon that haunted him for a decade. That playoff victory over Justin Rose last April completed the career Grand Slam and lifted an enormous psychological burden. The question now: does the monkey off his back unleash a new Rory, or was last year the summit? His 2026 form has been solid but not spectacular, with one win and four top-10s suggesting he's managing his schedule carefully for a title defense. The raw talent remains breathtaking -- nobody hits a golf ball quite like McIlroy when he's firing -- and defending at Augusta with the Grand Slam complete could be the ultimate pressure-free environment.

whyTheyWillWin:
Rory McIlroy returns to Augusta National as a fundamentally different golfer than the one who spent a decade chasing the career Grand Slam. The psychological transformation since last April's playoff victory over Justin Rose is profound and cannot be understated. For years, the weight of expectation at the Masters visibly affected McIlroy's body language, his decision-making, and his ability to close. That burden is gone. He is now the defending champion, the career Grand Slam holder, and a man with absolutely nothing left to prove at Augusta National. That freedom could be the most dangerous weapon in the field.

McIlroy's raw physical tools remain the best in the field for Augusta National. Nobody drives the golf ball like Rory -- his combination of 320-yard carry distance and fairway accuracy turns the par-5s into birdie and eagle opportunities that shorter hitters simply cannot access. Augusta's par-5s have historically decided the tournament, and McIlroy's scoring on those four holes is the best in the field. When he stripes his driver and pairs it with his explosive iron play, he can go on scoring runs that leave the rest of the field in the dust.

The defending champion dynamic works strongly in McIlroy's favor. He has the most recent competitive experience at Augusta under maximum pressure, and he handled it brilliantly. He knows exactly what it feels like to navigate the back nine on Sunday with the green jacket on the line, and he prevailed. At 36, McIlroy is a seasoned champion with the game, the course knowledge, and the emotional intelligence to become the first defending champion to successfully defend since Tiger Woods in 2002. The historical significance of that achievement would fuel a man who has always been driven by legacy.
whyTheyWontWin:
McIlroy's short game has been his kryptonite at Augusta for over a decade, and one great week doesn't erase years of evidence. His scrambling percentage of 58.4% is the lowest among the top-5 favorites, and Augusta's green complexes are the most punishing in championship golf. When McIlroy misses greens here -- and he will miss some -- his ability to get up and down from the gnarly collection areas and closely mown chipping zones has historically been inconsistent. One bad wedge shot into Rae's Creek or one chunked chip on the 10th can unravel an entire round.

There's also the question of motivation and intensity. Defending champions at Augusta have a notoriously poor record, with the complacency of having already achieved the goal sometimes dulling the competitive edge. McIlroy's 2026 season has been good but not great, suggesting he may be pacing himself rather than arriving with the desperate hunger that drove him last year. The narrative of the career Grand Slam chase provided extraordinary motivation -- without it, does McIlroy bring the same ferocity?

name: Xander Schauffele
narrative: Since breaking through at the 2024 PGA and Open, Schauffele has played with a different gear. Augusta has been his white whale -- five top-10s without a jacket. The putter is finally matching the ball-striking, and that's terrifying for everyone else. His all-around game has no glaring weakness, making him the most consistent contender in the field. With two majors now on his resume, the mental barrier that once held him back at Augusta has been shattered, and he arrives in 2026 as a complete player who knows he belongs at the top of any leaderboard.

whyTheyWillWin:
Xander Schauffele's path to a green jacket in 2026 is paved by the most complete game in professional golf. Unlike many contenders who rely on one dominant skill, Schauffele excels everywhere -- off the tee, with his irons, around the greens, and on them. His strokes gained total consistently ranks in the top three on Tour, and his ability to avoid bad holes is almost unmatched. At Augusta, where one quadruple bogey can end your tournament, Schauffele's consistency is a superpower.

The transformation in Schauffele's career since winning the 2024 PGA Championship and Open Championship cannot be overstated. For years, he was the best player without a major, and that label carried psychological weight. Now, with two majors on his resume, the pressure has been replaced by confidence. He knows he can close on the biggest stages, and that knowledge fundamentally changes how a player navigates the back nine on Sunday at Augusta. His scrambling around the greens -- the best among the favorites -- is particularly valuable when the Sunday pins are in their most diabolical positions.

Schauffele's five top-10 finishes at the Masters without a victory is often cited as a negative, but it's actually a profound positive. He knows this course intimately. He has experienced every wind condition, every pin position, every pressure moment that Augusta can produce. The only thing missing was the finishing kick, and his 2024 major breakthrough proved he has it. At 32, in the heart of his prime, with his game firing and his confidence at an all-time high, the green jacket that has eluded him feels less like an if and more like an inevitability.
whyTheyWontWin:
The elephant in the room is Augusta itself. Five top-10s without a win creates a narrative that this course, for whatever reason, doesn't quite fit Schauffele at the business end of the tournament. His Sunday scoring average at the Masters lags behind his performance on other days, and there's a question about whether Augusta's specific demands on Sunday -- the roars from ahead, the back-nine drama, the sheer weight of history -- affect him differently than other major venues.

Schauffele also lacks the elite distance off the tee that defines the modern Augusta contender. While he's far from short, he doesn't bomb it like Scheffler, Aberg, or DeChambeau, meaning his par-5 birdie rate is good but not elite. In a tournament that is increasingly won by players who can reach the par-5s with short irons, Schauffele's relative lack of length could be the difference between a good week and a green jacket.

name: Ludvig Åberg
narrative: The Swede nearly won the thing as a Masters rookie in 2024 and backed it up with another top-5 last year. At 26, Aberg has the length, the temperament, and the trajectory of a future champion. It's not a matter of if, but when -- and Augusta feels like where. His ball-striking numbers off the tee are the best in the field, combining elite distance with accuracy that belies his power. The Swedish sensation has handled the pressure of being golf's next big thing with remarkable composure, displaying a stoic Nordic calm that is perfectly suited to the cauldron of Masters Sunday. With two strong Augusta starts under his belt, the course knowledge gap that usually holds back young players has been significantly narrowed.

whyTheyWillWin:
Ludvig Aberg represents the most exciting combination of raw talent and Augusta-specific potential in the 2026 field. His strokes gained off the tee leads all 88 players, and at Augusta National -- where driving distance correlates more strongly with scoring than at any other major venue -- that advantage is worth nearly half a stroke per round before he even hits an approach shot. Aberg doesn't just hit it far; he hits it far and straight, which turns Augusta's par-5s into legitimate eagle holes and transforms the longer par-4s into wedge approaches.

What makes Aberg's candidacy especially compelling is how quickly he has mastered Augusta National. Most young players need four or five trips to understand the course's subtleties -- the optical illusions, the invisible slopes, the way the wind swirls through the trees on Amen Corner. Aberg finished runner-up in his very first Masters and followed it with a T5. That trajectory suggests a player whose talent is so overwhelming that it shortcuts the learning curve, and whose temperament is so steady that he doesn't get rattled by the enormity of the occasion. At 26, he displayed the composure of a seasoned veteran during both Sunday back nines.

The historical parallels are intriguing. Tiger Woods won his first Masters at 21, and Aberg possesses a similar combination of prodigious length and all-around brilliance that made Tiger's Augusta dominance inevitable. With two wins already in 2026 and a FedEx Cup ranking of third, Aberg is playing the best golf of his young career at precisely the right time. His elite driving, improving approach play, and growing comfort at Augusta create a profile that suggests the 2026 Masters could be the moment that launches a decade-long dynasty at Augusta National. The only question is timing -- and the timing feels perfect.
whyTheyWontWin:
Aberg's short game remains a genuine vulnerability that Augusta will expose without mercy. His scrambling percentage of 55.2% is the lowest among the top-5 favorites, and when you miss greens at Augusta -- even slightly -- the consequences are severe. The narrative power of a Spieth Masters victory in 2026 should not be dismissed as irrelevant. whyTheyWillWin:
Brooks Koepka has built his entire career around peaking at the biggest moments, and the Masters is as big as it gets. His five major championships are not the product of luck or favorable draws -- they reflect an athlete who fundamentally transforms when the stakes rise, who finds a gear that exists only in major championship weeks. Koepka's iron play from 175-225 yards, the most critical distance range at Augusta National, has historically been elite, and when he is dialed in off the tee, few players in the world can match his ability to shorten the par 5s that so often decide this tournament. His combination of power and precision in those middle distances is exactly what Augusta rewards.

The 2023 Masters, where Koepka finished solo second and led deep into Sunday before Jon Rahm's charge, proved he has the course knowledge and the game to contend at Augusta. That experience is invaluable -- he knows how to navigate Amen Corner's dangers, understands the subtle breaks on Augusta's treacherous greens from years of repetition, and has the length to take advantage of the reachable par 5s that separate contenders from pretenders. His driving distance, consistently in the top 15 on tour, gives him short irons into holes where others are hitting mid-irons, and that club differential compounds into a significant scoring advantage over four rounds.

Perhaps most importantly, Koepka thrives on being doubted. The narrative around his decline from the pinnacle of the sport fuels him in ways that are difficult to quantify but impossible to ignore. History has shown repeatedly that writing him off before a major is a mistake -- he came back from knee surgery to win the 2023 PGA Championship when few gave him a chance. His rivalry with the game's best players -- Scheffler, Rahm, DeChambeau -- pushes him to elevate his performance. If Koepka arrives at Augusta healthy, with his knee feeling strong and his iron game sharp from the spring LIV events, he possesses the rare combination of power, precision, and big-game mentality that can produce a green jacket. At 36, he knows his window is narrowing, and that urgency could be the final ingredient that transforms a good week into a historic one.
whyTheyWontWin:
The uncomfortable truth about Koepka's 2026 Masters candidacy is that his best golf may be behind him. His putting, which has always been the weakest part of his game, becomes a glaring liability on Augusta's lightning-fast, undulating greens where every putt requires precision and nerve. When the field's best putters are gaining half a stroke or more on the greens per round, Koepka losing strokes with the flat stick is a deficit that power alone cannot overcome. The greens at Augusta are the ultimate equalizer, and Koepka's putter is simply not equipped to compete with the likes of Scheffler, Schauffele, and Smith on those surfaces.

His body is also a genuine concern that cannot be dismissed. The knee surgeries have accumulated over the years, and Augusta National is one of the most physically demanding walks in championship golf with its dramatic elevation changes and relentless hills. The four consecutive days of competition and the mental intensity all take a toll that compounds when the body is compromised. His inconsistent results on the LIV circuit -- scattered top 10s mixed with uninspiring finishes well outside contention -- suggest the form simply is not there week to week. Hoping his game magically peaks for one specific week in April is more wishful thinking than strategic analysis, and the odds reflect that uncertainty.

whyTheyWillWin:
Russell Henley is the quintessential under-the-radar Masters contender whose game profile is statistically optimized for Augusta National. Henley hits greens in regulation at a rate that generates birdie opportunities, and when he does miss, his touch around the green limits the damage to pars rather than bogeys.

Henley's Masters trajectory shows a player who is quietly building toward a breakthrough. His T22 in 2023, T25 in 2024, and T10 in 2025 represent a steady upward curve of improving performance as he accumulates course knowledge with each appearance. The T10 last year was particularly encouraging because it demonstrated he can sustain competitive golf at Augusta for all four rounds, posting the kind of consistent, mistake-free scorecard that keeps a player within striking distance entering Sunday. Seven Masters appearances have given him a thorough understanding of the course's demands.

The case for Henley winning rests on his ability to post four steady rounds in the 68-69 range while the more explosive players in the field make mistakes chasing birdies. Augusta has a long history of rewarding the patient, disciplined player who avoids disaster while the leaders stumble. Henley's four top-10 finishes in nine events this season prove his game is in peak form, and his iron play is the kind of weapon that can produce a championship week if the putting cooperates.
whyTheyWontWin:
Henley's ceiling at Augusta is fundamentally limited by his lack of driving distance. The par-5s represent the most significant scoring opportunities at the Masters, and Henley's 0.20 strokes gained off the tee means he is frequently laying up or hitting long irons into par-5 greens while the bombers in the field are attacking with wedges. That distance disadvantage translates to a 2-3 stroke deficit on the par-5s alone across four rounds, a gap that his iron play cannot fully close.

Perhaps more concerning is Henley's complete absence of wins in 2026 and his lack of any major championship victories. The Masters demands a level of Sunday fortitude that can only be forged through experience winning under extreme pressure, and Henley has never demonstrated that ability on the biggest stage. His under-the-radar profile, while analytically appealing, reflects the reality that he has never truly contended for a major championship and remains unproven in the cauldron of Augusta's Sunday back nine.

His wedge play, consistently ranked among the top 10 on Tour throughout his career, is the exact skill set that allows players to attack Augusta's tucked pins and convert birdie opportunities that the rest of the field cannot access.

The JT redemption arc of 2026 is a story that fits the Masters perfectly. After struggling through a difficult stretch in 2023 and 2024 where his game lost its characteristic precision, Thomas has rebuilt his swing and his confidence from the ground up. His win earlier this season was not a fluke -- it was the culmination of months of technical refinement and mental recalibration. He arrives at Augusta with the hunger of a player who has something to prove and the experience of a player who has already proven it. Thomas's nine Masters appearances give him an intimate understanding of Augusta's architectural demands. He knows how to navigate the first-cut rough on the approach to the 11th green. name: Corey Conners
narrative: One of the purest ball-strikers on Tour, Conners' approach play is perfectly suited for Augusta's demanding second shots. If the putter ever shows up for four days, watch out.
keyStrengths: Elite approach play with 0.90 strokes gained on approach -- the highest in the entire Masters field and the single most predictive stat for Augusta success

whyTheyWillWin:
Corey Conners possesses the single most important skill for winning the Masters: elite iron play. His 0.90 strokes gained on approach is not just good -- it is the highest mark in the entire Masters field, surpassing even Scottie Scheffler in this critical category. Augusta National is, at its core, a second-shot golf course, and Conners hits those second shots better than anyone. His ability to control trajectory, spin rate, and landing angle with mid-irons gives him access to pin positions that the rest of the field must play away from. When he is flagging approach after approach into Augusta's elevated, undulating greens, the birdie opportunities accumulate in a way that can produce the 65s and 66s needed to win.

Conners' Augusta track record validates the statistical argument. His T10 in 2023 and T6 in 2024 demonstrated that his iron play translates directly into competitive results at this specific venue. The T6 was particularly notable because Conners was in genuine contention entering the final round, proving he can handle the pressure of a Masters Sunday. Six appearances have given him a thorough education in Augusta's architectural subtleties, and his disciplined course management -- he consistently avoids the big numbers that eliminate contenders -- provides a reliable scoring floor.

The Canadian's path to victory is clear and specific: if his putting improves from below-average to merely average for four days, his elite approach play will generate enough birdie opportunities to contend. Putting is the most variable stat in golf, and players frequently experience four-day stretches where the flatstick is dramatically better than their season average. If Conners gets one of those weeks at Augusta, his iron play will do the rest.
whyTheyWontWin:
The putter is the elephant in the room for Conners' Masters chances. At -0.10 strokes gained putting, he is actively losing ground to the field on the greens, and at Augusta, where the putting surfaces are the most consequential in championship golf, that liability becomes magnified. The best iron player in the field is irrelevant if he cannot convert the birdie opportunities his approach play creates, and Conners' putting numbers suggest he will leave multiple makeable putts short, wide, or burning edges throughout the week.

Conners also lacks the scoring explosiveness that modern Masters champions need. Without elite length off the tee, his par-5 scoring -- the primary separator at Augusta -- lags behind the bombers in the field. His ceiling on any given round is lower than players who can reach the par-5s in two with wedges, and that scoring differential compounds across four rounds into a 4-6 stroke disadvantage that no amount of iron play precision can overcome.

name: Cameron Smith
narrative: The best short game in professional golf meets the most demanding green complexes in the world -- that's always going to be a potent combination. Smith's flat stick wizardry nearly won him the jacket in 2022, and Augusta's greens reward the creativity he has in spades. A recent LIV win has his confidence trending upward, and his calm temperament under pressure makes him dangerous whenever he tees it up in a major.
keyStrengths: Arguably the best short game and scrambling ability in professional golf
Elite putting on fast, undulating greens -- exactly what Augusta demands
Course history includes a runner-up and third-place finish at the Masters
Creative shot-making ability unlocks scoring opportunities others cannot see
Proven major champion who closed out The Open at St Andrews under immense pressure
keyWeaknesses: Driving distance is a significant disadvantage against the longest hitters in the field
Accuracy off the tee can be inconsistent, leading to difficult recovery positions
Limited competitive reps against PGA Tour-caliber fields due to LIV schedule
whyTheyWillWin:
Cameron Smith possesses the single most important weapon for winning the Masters: the best short game in professional golf. Augusta National's green complexes are the most severe and creative in major championship golf, and Smith's ability to get up and down from seemingly impossible positions is unmatched in the modern game. His putting on fast, sloping greens -- the defining skill that separated him from the field at the 2022 Open Championship at St Andrews -- translates directly to the bentgrass surfaces at Augusta, where reading subtle breaks and committing to aggressive lines is the difference between birdies and bogeys. No player in the field sees more possibilities around the green than Smith.

Smith's Augusta resume already tells a compelling story that demands respect. His tie for second in 2020 and third-place finish in 2022 demonstrate that his game fits this course at an elite level. In 2022, he shot a Sunday 68 to finish just three shots behind Scottie Scheffler, and his ability to score on the par 5s -- despite lacking the raw distance of his rivals -- speaks to his creativity and instinctive scoring ability. He uses angles, trajectory, and touch to manufacture birdie opportunities that others try to overpower and often fail to convert. His approach to Augusta is fundamentally different from the bombers, and that creativity is itself a competitive advantage because it is nearly impossible to replicate.

The intangible factor with Smith is his temperament. The Australian plays with a calm, almost detached demeanor that serves him brilliantly under major championship pressure. He does not get rattled by Amen Corner's challenges or the deafening roars echoing through the Georgia pines when a rival makes birdie. His 2022 Open victory, where he fired a closing 64 to chase down Rory McIlroy at the home of golf, remains one of the great final-round performances in recent major history and proved he can go low when it matters most. If his tee-to-green game shows up at even an average level, his short game and putter can carry him to a green jacket.

A recent win on the LIV circuit has his confidence surging at the right time, and the reduced schedule means fresh legs and a clear mind for the most demanding week in golf. Smith is the kind of player who does not need to overpower Augusta -- he simply needs to outthink and outtouch it.
whyTheyWontWin:
Smith's Achilles heel is the same one it has always been: he simply does not hit it far enough to compete with the modern game's elite bombers at Augusta. The course has been lengthened repeatedly over the past two decades, and the gap between Smith's driving distance and players like Scheffler, DeChambeau, and Clark means he is hitting significantly longer clubs into par 4s and cannot always reach the par 5s in two. That distance deficit costs him strokes every round that even his magical short game cannot always recover, especially when the course is playing firm and fast.

The LIV factor also looms large over his preparation. Smith's competitive schedule is fundamentally different from the PGA Tour players he will face at Augusta. The 54-hole, no-cut, shotgun-start format does not replicate the pressure and rhythm of a 72-hole major championship, and his limited exposure to the strongest fields in golf means he may be underprepared for the sustained intensity of contending across four rounds at Augusta. His recent Masters results -- T25 in 2024 and T14 in 2025 -- suggest a player who is comfortable at Augusta but not quite sharp enough to win against the very best when it matters most on Sunday afternoon.

whyTheyWillWin:
Sungjae Im's case for the 2026 Masters is anchored in a single, undeniable fact: he has already proven he can contend at Augusta National at the very highest level. His second-place finish in the 2020 Masters, where he posted 11-under par and was in the final group on Sunday, demonstrated that his game fits the course and that he possesses the mental fortitude to handle Augusta's Sunday pressure. That runner-up finish was not a fluke born of a weak field or favorable conditions -- he finished second to Dustin Johnson, who posted one of the most dominant Masters performances in history. Im was simply the second-best player in the field that week, and that pedigree separates him from the majority of dark horses.

Im's consistency at Augusta across six appearances is remarkable for a player who receives relatively little attention in Masters preview coverage. His finishes of 2nd, T14, T14, T16, T14, and T18 represent the kind of sustained competence that suggests a genuine course fit rather than occasional good fortune. He hits a high percentage of greens in regulation, his iron play is reliable under pressure, and his even-keeled temperament is perfectly suited to a course that punishes emotional decision-making. While flashier names produce higher highs and lower lows, Im grinds out competitive rounds with mechanical precision.

The 2026 season has been Im's strongest in years, with a win and four top-10 finishes suggesting he has found another gear. His strokes gained approach of 0.55 ranks among the best in the field, and his putting has shown signs of improvement that could be the missing piece. If Im can replicate the putting form he showed during his 2020 runner-up finish -- when he gained over a stroke per round on the greens -- his tee-to-green consistency becomes a championship-winning combination.
whyTheyWontWin:
The fundamental concern with Im's Masters chances is that his game profile is built for consistency rather than dominance, and the Masters increasingly rewards the latter. His strokes gained total of 1.20, while solid, places him behind at least a dozen players in the field, and his inability to produce the kind of explosive low rounds that modern Masters champions need on Saturday and Sunday limits his realistic ceiling. Im's best Masters finish required him to play nearly perfect golf for four days, and even then he finished five shots behind the winner.

Since his brilliant 2020 runner-up, Im's Masters results have plateaued in the T14-to-T18 range -- respectable but not threatening. The course seems to have figured out a ceiling for his game, and without a significant improvement in his putting or scoring ability, breaking through that ceiling to challenge for the green jacket appears unlikely against a field loaded with more talented ball-strikers and more explosive scorers.

whyTheyWillWin:
Keegan Bradley arrives at the 2026 Masters riding the greatest wave of momentum in his career, fueled by the transformative experience of captaining the victorious 2025 United States Ryder Cup team. That captaincy did something that fifteen years of professional golf had not fully accomplished: it cemented Bradley's belief that he belongs among the game's elite. The confidence, leadership skills, and big-stage composure he developed during the Ryder Cup have translated directly into his playing career, producing a win and three top-10 finishes in 2026. Bradley is playing with the swagger of a man who has nothing left to prove and everything left to win.

Bradley's 2011 PGA Championship victory provides a psychological foundation that most of the field cannot match. He has stood on the 72nd tee of a major championship needing to execute, and he has delivered. That experience, combined with nine Masters appearances that have given him extensive course knowledge, creates a profile of a player who is both battle-tested and Augusta-educated. His iron play and approach numbers have been quietly solid throughout 2026, and his competitive toughness -- the ability to grind out pars when the course is fighting back -- is a trait that Augusta rewards.

At 39, Bradley represents the archetype of the late-blooming Masters champion. Phil Mickelson was 33 when he won his first green jacket. Fred Couples was 32. Tiger Woods won his fifth at 43. The Masters has a long history of rewarding experience, patience, and accumulated course knowledge over raw physical talent, and Bradley possesses all three in abundance. His bulldog mentality, combined with the emotional high of his Ryder Cup success, makes him a dangerous outsider who could shock the field.
whyTheyWontWin:
Bradley's Masters record across nine appearances is, frankly, uninspiring. A best finish of T12 at a course where he has played 36 rounds suggests a fundamental mismatch between his game and Augusta's architectural demands. The par-5s, where the field's longest hitters gain their decisive advantages, represent scoring opportunities that Bradley cannot fully exploit due to his modest driving distance. His approach play, while solid, has not produced the kind of proximity-to-hole numbers at Augusta that translate into birdie conversion.

The concern with Bradley is that his current momentum may be more narrative than substance. His Ryder Cup captaincy was a wonderful career moment, but translating the emotional high of team competition into individual major championship performance is not straightforward. His 2026 season numbers, while improved, still rank him outside the top 20 on Tour in most statistical categories, and the Masters demands a level of excellence that Bradley has rarely sustained for four consecutive days.

name: Wyndham Clark
narrative: Clark's raw power and improving course management have made him a legitimate threat at Augusta. The 2023 US Open winner has learned how to navigate major pressure, and his upward trajectory at the Masters -- MC to T14 to T8 in three consecutive years -- suggests a big week is coming. Proven major champion who closed out a US Open under intense Sunday pressure

whyTheyWillWin:
Wyndham Clark is the prototype of the modern Masters champion: long off the tee, improving rapidly, and armed with the confidence of a major championship victory. His driving distance, consistently inside the top 10 on the PGA Tour, gives him a massive structural advantage at Augusta National, where the par 5s are the primary scoring holes. While others are laying up or hitting fairway woods into these greens, Clark is attacking with short irons, creating eagle and birdie opportunities that compound over four rounds into a decisive edge. In a field where par-5 scoring separates contenders from also-rans, Clark's length is not just an asset -- it is a cheat code.

Clark's trajectory at Augusta is the most encouraging storyline of any player in this tier. A missed cut in 2023, a T14 in 2024, and a T8 in 2025 -- that is not luck, that is a player systematically learning one of golf's most complex courses. Each year, he has gained a deeper understanding of where to miss, how the greens slope, and which pins to attack versus which demand patience. His 2023 US Open victory at Los Angeles Country Club proved he can handle the mental gauntlet of a major championship, executing under pressure when others crumble. That experience has clearly translated to his improved Masters performances, as he now approaches Augusta with the calm confidence of a proven champion rather than the nervous energy of a hopeful contender.

The physical element cannot be overlooked. Clark is one of the fittest players on tour, a former collegiate athlete who brings genuine athleticism to a course that punishes fatigue. Augusta's dramatic elevation changes and the cumulative toll of four rounds in April weather demand physical resilience, and Clark has it in abundance. His conditioning allows him to maintain swing speed and focus deep into Sunday afternoon, when other players are grinding through fatigue. Combined with a season that has already produced a win and four top 10s, he arrives at the 2026 Masters with form, fitness, and a burning desire to add a green jacket to his US Open trophy. At 32, he is entering his peak years, the swing is maturing, and the timing feels right for a player whose entire career has been building toward this moment.
whyTheyWontWin:
Three Masters appearances is a thin resume at a course that historically rewards deep experience. Augusta National reveals its secrets slowly, and players typically need five or more starts before they truly understand the subtleties of the greens, the sight lines, and the strategic nuances that separate good weeks from great ones. Clark is still on that learning curve, and the players above him on the oddsboard have years more institutional knowledge. He may not yet know the specific pin positions that demand caution, or the angles of approach that turn good shots into great ones, and those gaps in knowledge can cost two or three strokes over 72 holes.

His short game remains a vulnerability that Augusta will ruthlessly expose. When he misses greens -- and he will, because Augusta's green complexes are the most demanding in golf -- his scrambling percentage suggests he will drop shots that more creative scramblers like Cameron Smith will save. Augusta punishes aggressive misses with pitches and chips from difficult lies to fast, sloping greens, and Clark's around-the-green game is not yet refined enough to consistently save par from those positions. The combination of limited experience and a short game that is not yet elite makes him more likely to post another strong top-10 finish than to break through for the victory.

whyTheyWillWin:
Dustin Johnson's 2020 Masters victory, where he annihilated the field by five strokes at 20-under par, remains one of the most dominant performances in tournament history and serves as a permanent reminder of what DJ is capable of when everything clicks. His natural length off the tee, even at 41, remains prodigious enough to overpower Augusta's par-5s, and his effortless swing -- perhaps the most naturally gifted action in the history of the game -- is capable of producing four rounds of elite ball-striking without warning.

The argument for DJ rests on the premise that Augusta National is the one course in the world where muscle memory and institutional knowledge can overcome form. Johnson has played Augusta 13 times. He knows every slope, every bounce, every subtle break on every green. He knows that the second shot on 11 plays longer than it looks, that the tee shot on 13 needs to start at the right edge of the trees, that the pin position on 16 dictates whether you aim at the left bunker or the right slope. That accumulated wisdom does not disappear because your strokes gained total has dropped -- it lives in your hands and your eyes and your feet.

If DJ were to catch fire with the putter for four days -- which he has done before at Augusta, most notably in 2020 when his putting was otherworldly -- his natural length and ball-striking quality are still sufficient to compete with anyone in the field. The LIV narrative would add an electrifying subplot to the tournament, and DJ's laid-back demeanor under pressure has always been one of his greatest assets.
whyTheyWontWin:
The honest assessment of Dustin Johnson's 2026 Masters chances is that they are slim. His overall game has declined significantly since joining LIV Golf, with a strokes gained total of just 0.55 that would not be competitive in most PGA Tour fields, let alone at a major championship. His putting, which was the catalyst for his 2020 victory, has deteriorated to a -0.15 strokes gained liability that Augusta's greens will exploit mercilessly. Three consecutive Masters finishes outside the top 30 confirm that the decline is real and accelerating.

The lack of regular competition against elite fields on LIV Golf has dulled the competitive edge that once made Johnson one of the most feared closers in golf. At 41, the physical tools are diminishing, and the motivation to grind through four grueling days at Augusta may not match the fire that burned during his peak years.
